Okay, rule 20, always know that you don't truly provide a service until you close, okay? You understand? You're not providing the service by selling them, presenting, demonstrating, promoting, marketing, building trust. These things are very commendable, good for you, admirable actions, but look, in reality, they don't compare to the close. When you close, you provide a service for the first time. And you finally say, they say yes, you say yes, you guys exchange probably money for a service or a product, then you become a value to the customer. Then your product can be used by that customer. Look, breathing and eating are important, but ultimately, you want to live. Okay, you don't want to go, I got to breathe, I got to eat, okay? No, man, you're trying to live, right? See the same with selling and closing. You know, selling is critical. Look, you do it because you want to close. You have to build value in your product. You have to create a relationship. You've got to build trust. You need to demonstrate and build the value benefit proposition of both your product and whatever offer you're making. The close allows you and your company to expand. We talked about creating economies. The close creates economies for you and others. All the things that took place, all the steps that took place, maybe the days, the hours, the minutes, the months, the years for some of you, that took place prior to you closing, they were just necessary. It was necessary to get to the close, but those steps, no matter how many of them there were or how long it took, will not be the things that create your expansion and survival. Close the deal. Be willing to do whatever it takes to close the deal. And know that only until you actually get to that critical survival exchange point do you provide true value. So if you ask that to me, hey, man, Grant, I went and did this guy. We did a great presentation. We went to his house. I spent two hours with him briefing him. So what? There's no value in any of that. You educated him. Until you close somebody, if I put a kid in class, until he learns that, okay, you're the material, can apply it in the world and basically go out there and get a job, what I gave him was not of any real value. Until it can be exchanged with the rest of the world, there is no real value. And the only way to do that is to close. The close is that service that is of real value to your customer. So go out and close.
